data.title: Constellation Energy: A Growth-Oriented Nuclear Buy In A Power-Hungry Economy
data.description:
Thesis

I'm keeping my "BUY" recommendation on Constellation Energy (CEG) because I believe it is the only large cap option that offers exposure to carbon-free electric power in the United States.

I've covered the company before, and I'm still bullish because the investment case is not only intact but is reinforced by structural developments in energy consumption.

CEG has some qualities that give it a growth profile in what's typically seen as a defensive sector, such as nuclear scale, solid assets, and strong cash generation.

What makes CEG different is not just that it is nuclear-based but the fact that it operates as a merchant power producer.

Unlike regulated utilities with capped returns, the company plays directly in energy pricing.

Company Overview: What CEG Really Is

Constellation Energy is the biggest nuclear power operator in the U.S. It has a portfolio of units that operate continuously, day and night, regardless of the weather or fluctuations in other energy sources.

These units deliver power you can actually count on. This is especially important in a grid that is often left hanging by a thread from renewable energy sources.

As I see it, CEG's assets are not just important, but they are strategically irreplaceable.

What makes the story more interesting is the company's business model. CEG sells its energy in competitive electricity markets where revenues are directly affected by demand and prices.

This means greater volatility but also real potential for profit growth when consumption increases.

According to my perspective, this flexibility is part of the reason why CEG is valued as a growth platform and not just a utility.

In recent developments, as I will analyze below, CEG has deepened this story of stability and strategic value.

The company completed the acquisition of Calpine and created the largest power generation portfolio in the U.S. with the expansion of its energy infrastructure base (natural gas, hydro, and geothermal, beyond its nuclear fleet).

This move strengthens the scale and revenue stability, which I see as a major strategic progress in the effort to meet growing electricity demand.

The NRC (Nuclear Regulatory Commission) approved a $167 million digital modernization project at the Limerick Clean Energy Center, improving reliability, diagnostics, and grid security resiliency.

These technology upgrades surround the production with durability and long-term sustainability, which I consider important for a company that promises continuity and stability.

This, I am convinced, gives CEG not just revenue but a strategic role in the global AI energy ecosystem.

From my point of view, these developments show a company profile that uses the nuclear base as a growth platform for greater scale and diversification.

This flexibility in the business model is one of my main personal arguments in favor of CEG in this investment category.

Market Context: Why Nuclear Is Back

Now let's talk about the energy market.

The energy market in the United States and internationally is in a phase of structural change, where the demand for electricity is growing at a pace that was not expected a few years ago.

According to estimates of the U.S. Energy Information Administration, total electricity consumption in the United States will break new records and is expected to continue to grow until 2027.

A big part of this increase comes from data centers and other energy-intensive technological applications.

From a global perspective, the International Energy Agency reports that total electricity demand grew by 4% in 2024, much faster than the average in recent years, mainly due to increasing electrification, digitalization, and electrical applications.

One of the most powerful drivers of this growth is the rapid expansion of data centers (especially those supporting artificial intelligence applications).

Studies show that data center electricity consumption could double or even triple by 2028 putting significant strain on the existing power grid and adding needs that traditional infrastructure struggles to meet.

At the same time, renewable energy sources like solar and wind depend on weather conditions and day/night cycles.

This in turn increases the need for steady generation sources to ensure adequate power across the full spectrum of demand.

Nuclear energy fills this gap by offering carbon-free generation with a stable power supply regardless of weather or operating hours and therefore acts as a reliable foundation in an energy system facing increasing needs.

In this context, CEG sits right at the center of this shift as it has installed and operational assets without the need for multiyear investments or permitting of new projects.

Personally, I think this advantage remains undervalued by the market, given the outlook for larger and sustained electricity demand.

Financials & Earnings Power: Beyond Utility Thinking

The company announced strong EBITDA and Free Cash Flow while EBITDA grew by 15%-20%.

This development is linked to the rise in electricity demand and the company's position in the baseload market. If you ask me, this is the central pillar of the investment case.

The hedging strategy offers clear short term profit visibility, as a significant part of the production is already secured at known prices.

At the same time, a substantial percentage of production is exposed to upward movements in energy prices.

Management demonstrates discipline in capital allocation with share buyback programs that have reduced the number of shares by a mid-single digit percentage in recent years.

This strategy enhances value per share and supports long term EPS growth, and I see it as a sign of confidence in the sustainability and quality of cash flows.

In Q32025, the results helped me form a clearer and more measurable view of CEG's growth story in a measurable and not theoretical way.

The company announced EPS of $3.04 and GAAP EPS of $2.97, with adjusted EPS up $0.30 year-over-year, which I attribute primarily to better operational execution.

In my opinion, the strongest point of the quarter is the nuclear operation. It has a capacity factor of 96.8%, which is 4% above the average of the sector. At the same time, the company tightened its 2025 guidance to $9.05-$9.45 per share on a standalone basis. This gives confidence for the rest of the year.

From an investor’s perspective, what stands out is CEG’s ability to combine strong current operating performance with substantial financial flexibility, highlighted by approximately $14 billion in liquidity expected post-Calpine, which supports capital returns, balance sheet resilience, and continued execution of its long-term growth strategy.

Valuation: Paying For Quality And Growth

Constellation Energy's valuation already embeds a premium versus traditional utilities, a premium I consider justified given the asset quality, nuclear base, and growth profile.

At the current share price of $333, CEG trades at approximately 35x forward earnings, based on the midpoint of its 2025 standalone EPS guidance of $9.25.

I use P/E as the primary valuation framework because the company has transitioned into a phase of strong and predictable profitability supported by recurring cash flows.

In the bear case, I assume earnings growth moderates and valuation compresses toward 28x earnings, still a premium to regulated utilities but more consistent with a market pricing in higher volatility and slower contracting momentum.

Applying a 28x multiple to $9.50 of normalized earnings results in a target price of approximately $265, representing roughly 20% downside from current levels.

In the base case, I assume steady earnings growth in line with management guidance, continued capital discipline, and no major acceleration in hyperscaler contracting.

Under these assumptions, I view a 38x multiple as reasonable, reflecting CEG’s superior asset quality and merchant exposure.

Applying this multiple to earnings of roughly $9.60 supports a price target near $365, or about 10% upside.

In the bull case, accelerating demand from AI-driven data centers and long-term power purchase agreements materially improve earnings visibility and quality.

In this scenario, CEG increasingly resembles a long-duration growth compounder within the energy sector.

I apply a 49x multiple to $9.50–$9.70 of earnings power, resulting in a target price of approximately $465, implying upside of about 40%.

In general, CEG has limited downside and good upside, based on long-term drivers.

For that reason, I keep a "BUY" recommendation and see the premium as fair for a quality growth company.

The stock already shows this positive trend in the market. It has been going up for years and is up about 640% in the last four years.

The 18% drop from the highs gives a first entry point. The second one is near the 260 area.

Risks: What Could Go Wrong

The most critical risks for Constellation have to do with the regulatory environment and the nature of nuclear generation.

As we know, nuclear facilities face strict oversight, and any shift in regulation or licensing can increase costs and hurt profitability.

Additionally, political support for nuclear is more positive, but it can still change with social and political events.

Another key risk is the unexpected shutdown of the nuclear plants. This could reduce production, increase costs, and worsen the financial results. Volatility of the electricity prices creates uncertainty too, as it affects the revenues of a merchant company.

Conclusion

The most critical risks for Constellation have to do with the regulatory environment and the nature of nuclear generation.

As we know, nuclear facilities face strict oversight, and any shift in regulation or licensing can increase costs and hurt profitability.

Additionally, political support for nuclear is more positive, but it can still change with social and political events.

Another key risk is the unexpected shutdown of the nuclear plants.

This could reduce production, increase costs, and worsen the financial results. Volatility of the electricity prices creates uncertainty too, as it affects the revenues of a merchant company.

data.title: ATI: A Stock With Potential But Prudent Timing Is Advised
data.description:
Investment Thesis

ATI Inc. (ATI) is an interesting opportunity in the aerospace and defense segment, with significant sector tailwinds as the Trump administration is massively ramping up spending in defense, and in particular in US manufacturers. ATI specialises in titanium production, as well as the production of nickel alloys, titanium alloys, and other types of alloys essential to the US defense. These alloys are critical for US defense jet engines, being of national interest. The largest global producer and supplier of the US of titanium is China, and the US, the EU and Japan classify titanium as a “critical mineral.”

Securing the supply chain of titanium all the way until the jet engine production is of national interest for the US. The Australian company IperionX Limited (IPX) rallied in 2025 in expanding its titanium mining efforts within US borders. ATI is a little bit further down the line of the supply chain, where it uses the raw titanium to transform it into alloys and produce jet engines in solid partnerships with the Western duopoly for planes, The Boeing Company (BA) and Airbus SE (EADSF) (EADSY). The company clearly has strong tailwinds and a solid customer base, but while fundamentals are solid, the valuation is somewhat stretched, and I will rate ATI a “Buy,” but a buy on the next dip.

Business Overview

ATI’s core business is not per se tied to US national interest. It’s a company with a very high barrier business, limited competition, and also a limited customer base (primarily Boeing and Airbus). It supplies high-performance commercial and military engines, which have over the years turned into elements of national interest as the tensions, in particular trade wars, caused a series of import-export restrictions between China and the US (critical earth minerals, semiconductor chips, etc.).

The importance of ATI in the Aerospace & Defense sector has translated into its financials in the last two years. Indeed, the earnings of the company, as reflected by its stock price, were relatively flat from 1996 to 2022, and started to rally only in the aftermath of the Ukraine-Russia war that disrupted many supply chains.

The stock hit its first all-time high in 2026, its first all-time high since 2007, as sector tailwinds and a shift in global supply dynamics significantly improved the company’s fundamentals, turning it into an essential company for the US and Western aerospace. The company has turned into an important player as a supplier of jet engines (39% of ATI revenue as per the latest Q3 2025 earnings report), airframes, and new defense programs requiring high performance materials.

Hard Figures

The strong demands for the components mentioned above have translated into solid financials, with strong revenue and earnings growth reported in Q3 2025 (the one for Q4 is expected in February). The company reported 7% YoY revenue growth (above its 5-year average revenue growth of 5%) and a 42% spike in earnings per share with a $0.85 per share reported. It’s a relatively profitable company despite the high capex (high barrier) segment with a net income margin of 9.7%, again above its 5-year average when the company was primarily in loss with a -5.6% negative net income margin.

Return on equity exploded to 25%, up from its -7% 5-year average ROE, demonstrating a company’s fundamentals can change rapidly when the macro picture aligns with the business. The change in leadership with the new CEO Kimberly Fields (CEO since 1st of July, 2024) is also a shift with her emphasis on profitability and focus on business segments where demand (and growth potential) is higher. The segment growing the fastest (Jet Engine) is interestingly also the largest by revenue share (39%), demonstrating a common-sense strategy of the new CEO.

On another note, the company does not pay dividends (yet), meaning it’s not bound yet by dividend payments, allowing it some leeway and favoring discretionary share repurchases. Management indeed clearly prefers share repurchases and repurchased approximately two million shares for $150 million in Q3 alone, and an additional $120 million remains available on the current authorization. This brings the total share repurchase YTD to 6.4 million shares for $470 million.

The balance sheet of the company is relatively pristine despite years of financial losses and a capex-heavy segment. With a comfortable cash pile of $372 million, total assets of $5.0 billion surpass the $3.2 billion of total liabilities, allowing the company some leeway in its capital deployment in further share buybacks and strategic capex spending in the business segments with the highest growth rates.

A Stretched Valuation

While the fundamentals appear particularly solid, the financials are healthy, and the overall global picture favors US-based suppliers to the Western customers Boeing and Airbus amidst trade restrictions, the valuation is perhaps the only point that keeps me from rating the stock a Strong Buy.

Indeed, ATI is trading at a lofty 39x 2025 earnings and even on a forward basis is trading at 32x 2026 earnings, reflecting high earnings growth priced in but also a valuation more akin to tech growth stocks. Needless to mention, the stock is trading at significantly higher multiples than its 5-year historical average of 21x earnings and 1.5x sales, with the stock now hitting a P/S of 3.8x. From a P/B, the stock is also trading at 9.9x, above its 5.1x 5-year historical average. Of course, fundamentals have changed in a significant way, and the company is now profitable and growing, while previously it was a loss maker with flatline revenue. A good comparable in the absence of US peers producing the same alloys for the same customers is the sector median of Aerospace and Defense. According to Seeking Alpha data, the sector median for the sector is closer to 26x earnings and 2x price to sales. While it’s unlikely ATI will lose its momentum in the near future given the strength of the tailwinds favoring its niche, I would, as an investor, wait for pullbacks along the trajectory to accumulate more shares.

Key Risk Factors to My Buy Case

There are two key risk factors that could invalidate my bullish stance on the stock: one is supply-related, and the other is demand related.

Indeed, while the demand is currently strong (and assuming it remains strong backed by multiyear tailwinds), there is simply only so much ATI can process and execute. In other terms, the production capacity as well as the operational expansion are constrained, especially if ATI wants to maintain quality and avoid any adverse media in relation to the jet engines supplied to Boeing and Airbus, but also other product lines. To a large extent, the way ATI manages to expand its operations will dictate the growth pace of the business.

The other key risk factor is the cut in defense spending. While the conversation has been muted somewhat for a few months, one of the early hopes of the Trump administration was to cut defense spending in half (if Russia and China did so as well). However, this factor is mitigated in my view, as he called for a defense spending increase of 50% just last week.

Bottom Line

ATI is a solid company with the stars clearly aligned to support its growth and further stock price appreciation. The stark valuation is the key reason I am rating the stock a Buy instead of a Strong Buy, and in the absence of identical peers, any adverse news relative to these tailwinds could significantly reprice the stock. I expect some pullback after a +123% rally in the last twelve months, which could offer a great price entry (or accumulation if you are already a shareholder) in a stock that could continue outperforming the broader sector and markets.

data.title: Corning: Executing Well, But The Stock Is Priced Well Ahead Of It
data.description:
I’m initiating coverage on Corning Incorporated (GLW) at a time when the business is doing many things right. Over the past year, the company has really tightened its execution, improved margins, and repositioned its growth profile.

Today, its Optical Communications segment is still the dominant driver of revenue and profit growth. Even more importantly, though, is how its Springboard plan is showing up in the numbers. It certainly looks like it’s been a major success, one that is crucial to how I view the company moving forward.

Having said that, I’m going with a hold on Corning’s stock for now, and the issue is timing. In my view, the stock reflects a huge part of that improvement already. If you’re holding the stock today, it means that you have really high expectations of what Corning can and will do over the next few years, and the margin for error is very narrow.

At nearly $90/share, I believe that the valuation is full, and the combination of everything I have listed above makes this a good moment to step back, lay out what Corning actually is today, and decide whether the current setup justifies fresh capital.

Company Overview

According to most sources, along with the company’s own documents, Corning is the world’s biggest materials science and advanced manufacturing company by revenue, making fiber, cable, and multi-fiber connectors, among other things. It made ~$12 billion in net sales in the first three quarters of FY25.

Side note: Before covering it, I only really knew the company as the manufacturer of the Gorilla Glass that comes with all my Samsung Galaxy devices (I’m a big Galaxy aficionado, no Apple for me).

Corning runs five distinct operating segments, although it reports operations for its emerging businesses (solar & semiconductor technologies, along with pharmaceutical technologies & emerging innovations) under the Hemlock and Emerging Growth Businesses umbrella. More importantly, though, the company’s business revolves around selling critical components into industries where reliability, scale, and long qualification cycles matter more than speed.

In my opinion, the business today looks meaningfully different from how many investors may think about it, especially compared to a few years ago.

For one, Optical Communications is now the largest segment. This includes the fiber, cable, and connectivity solutions that it sells into telecom networks, broadband builds, enterprise infrastructure, and increasingly, AI-driven data centers.

Display Technologies is the next biggest contributor, but it doesn’t define the company anymore. This segment supplies glass to TV, monitor, and laptop panel makers. From the look of things, demand in this segment is mature and cyclical (there are only so many devices and screens we can look at daily), and I think that management will focus more on pricing discipline and margin protection rather than volume growth moving forward.

Then, there is the Specialty Materials segment. This is where Corning makes the Gorilla Glass and the other advanced glass products that are used primarily in premium consumer electronics.

Automotive (formerly Environmental Technologies) supplies the ceramic substrates and filters used in emissions control systems for automotive and heavy-duty vehicles.

Finally, we have the Life Sciences segment, the smallest one. This segment sells labware and cell culture products into biopharma, academic, and industrial labs.

Corning’s Moat And Growth Drivers

When I zoom out, Corning’s product stack is incredibly broad, but the monetization logic is consistent. Since it sells components that sit deep inside customer systems, they tend to stick around for years because the qualification cycles are long and switching costs are real. Think about the company’s $2.5 billion investment from Apple to make 100% of all the glass used for the latter’s iPhones and Apple Watches globally over the next few years in its Kentucky plant. Obviously, the deal has a lot more to it than just the optics of two long-time partners renewing a relationship. There’s the geopolitical benefit of having production closer to home for Apple, given its exposure in Asia, but it demonstrates something else for Corning: staying power. It’s just one example, but deals like these are what give the business its durability. At the same time, it also means that its growth is tied to customer capex cycles and end-market demand, not to quick wins.

In my opinion, Optical Communications is the clearest example. This segment sells fiber and connectivity solutions into two demand pools that don’t move the same way: telecom carriers and broadband builders on one side, and enterprise and hyperscale customers on the other. We’re seeing a difference in the current cycle because enterprise demand is being pulled forward by the higher fiber density requirements and new GenAI-related connectivity needs. If you look at the company’s Q3 FY25 earnings report, you’ll see that its Optical enterprise sales grew 58% year over year in Q3 because of strong adoption of its GenAI products.

It’s a different story for Display Technologies too, now. Corning sells primarily to a small number of large panel makers, and those volumes are still cyclical. I’d say that the segment behaves more like a cash engine and margin stabilizer than a growth engine, and the Q3 numbers reflect that dynamic.

Specialty Materials is where Corning’s premium glass relationships show up. Customers here care about durability and optical performance, and when you win a design, it is typically going to stay for several years. I’m thinking about Samsung while writing this, but it’s also easy to see that Apple’s ecosystem is central to how many investors think about this segment. I mentioned the $2.5 billion commitment earlier, and the word from management seems to be that the deal is an incremental growth opportunity through 2026 and beyond.

Looking at the last three major segments, I’d describe Automotive as a steady, regulation-influenced business. Once it wins a platform, the content is relatively stable over a vehicle program, so the story here is usually more about steady execution than upside surprise. The same goes for Life Sciences, although the demand there comes from consumables and lab demand. It’s not a growth engine, but it does its job well for Corning’s overall profile.

Hemlock & Emerging Growth Businesses is the interesting segment, in my opinion. Here, the sales have mostly come from solar technology, and management is very confident that it can turn its solar business into a $2.5 billion sales machine by 2028. It made $200 million in sales in Q1 FY25, and the figure was around that mark in Q2. In Q3, though, it was well over $300 million, although some of that came from semiconductor sales. Still, we can see the size of the opportunity there.

On the Q3 earnings call, management said that it plans to produce well over 1 million wafers daily in Q4 FY25 after it finished building “the largest solar ingot and wafer facility in the United States, co-located with our polysilicon manufacturing facility in Hemlock, Michigan.” That’s not a small announcement, and when you factor in the fact that it has customers committed to using over 80% of its capacity over the next 5 years, it’s hard not to believe that it can hit the $2.5 billion target in 2028.

Springboard Is The Bridge

Springboard is Corning’s execution system for turning these demand trends into real financial outcomes. The plan centers on two outcomes by the end of 2026:

  1. Add over $4 billion in annualized sales, and

  2. Reach a 20% operating margin.

The company started this plan in Q4 FY23, and where it gets interesting is that Springboard is already showing results much earlier than planned. In the Q3 2025 earnings release, management said the company has added $4 billion to its annualized sales run rate and expects to reach the 20% operating margin target in Q4 2025, a full year ahead of schedule.

The new plan is to hit $6 billion by Q4 2026, and at this rate, it is definitely feasible. But a lot has to keep going right. So when I talk about Corning’s growth and operating profiles changing, I’m not talking about a new identity. I’m talking about a portfolio where growth and tighter execution are now doing the heavy lifting, and the company’s own operating system (Springboard) is translating that into margin and cash flow improvements. Now, let’s look at the numbers.

The Numbers Are Great

In my opinion, the financials back up most of what management is saying, but they also define the boundaries of the opportunity, for now. The cleanest way to see what has changed is to look at core sales progression through FY25, which strips out the noise so we can see the business that Corning actually manages.

Core sales trend (FY25)

Quarter

Core Sales

Q1 FY25

~$3.7B

Q2 FY25

~$4.0B

Q3 FY25

~$4.3B

In the table above, we can see that sales increase sequentially across all three quarters, and it is a clear progression from FY2024. So, it’s not a rebound. More importantly, the growth is coming from product mix and pricing, although Optical Communications is doing most of the work.

Optical Communications revenue trend (FY25)

Quarter

Revenue (approx.)

YoY Growth

Q1 FY25

~$1.35B

40%+

Q2 FY25

~$1.57B

~40%

Q3 FY25

~$1.65B

~33%

Growth was a bit more moderate in Q3, but that is exactly what you would expect as the base expands. The crucial thing is that the demand remains broad and sustained rather than dependent on a single customer or project because everything else flows from that.

The margins expanded steadily across the year, with the core operating margin reaching 19.6% in Q3. That confirms Springboard is delivering, as I outlined earlier, but it also tells us that from here, incremental expansion becomes harder, especially once Corning’s pricing actions annualize. The same goes for the company’s free cash flow. It improved along with the margins, and that improves the earnings quality. However, it doesn’t turn Corning into a structurally high-return business. The company will still spend a ton of capital by virtue of its business model, and I expect cash generation to always move with cycles, even if those cycles are now better diversified.

For me, the main takeaway from these numbers is that Corning is executing well, and the operational improvement is real. Also, the growth mix is healthier than it has been in years, and now, we can see how much of that improvement is visible. That last point is the one that matters most for the stock, so let’s look at the valuation next.

Valuation: Quality Is Up, Price Has Moved Faster

I firmly believe that Corning’s execution over the past year justifies a re-rating. The problem is that the stock has already taken most of that re-rating upfront.

At current levels, the stock is trading at multiples that imply that this is a business with higher structural growth and lower cyclicality than Corning has historically delivered. That may be true long-term, but we still have to consider what exists in the here and now.

Metric

Corning (GLW)

P/E (TTM)

~58x

P/E (FWD)

~49x

EV/EBITDA (FWD)

~19x

EV/Sales

~5x

These aren’t “wrong” multiples if Corning were a structurally high-growth, asset-light business. But the company is still capital-intensive and has high exposure to enterprise and hyperscaler capex cycles. It also relies on pricing discipline in mature segments like Display.

Today, Corning is trading closer to high-quality infrastructure or industrial tech names than to traditional materials companies, and I don’t think that its return profile has fully crossed that line yet. It is still largely reliant on one or two main growth engines, albeit the potential returns from those can almost justify a premium on the stock. But it is also near peak margins a year ahead of schedule, and when a stock prices in that combination, I’d say the upside becomes more incremental than asymmetric.

Let’s look at the potential revenue total for FY25. Today, the reported figure for Q1-Q3 is around $12 billion, and if Q4 delivers the $4.35 billion, the total will come to around $16.3 billion. That means the P/E multiples (TTM and FWD) will compress; the latter down to maybe the high 30s or low 40s. The same goes for its EV/EBITDA multiples. At the same time, we will have improved visibility into its forward earnings, but not enough to value it like a structurally high-growth business. It will still command a big premium over its peers, and Q4 will just be defending today’s price, not creating new upside.

I’m not arguing Corning is overvalued in isolation. I’m saying the price now assumes that the good outcomes will persist for an extended time, and there is limited allowance for Optical’s growth normalizing or the margin plateauing after pricing actions annualize. There’s also the risk of a typical industrial downcycle, and that setup doesn’t give me a margin of safety.

Risks & Catalysts

For Corning Incorporated, the key risk right now is the valuation meeting reality. The stock is priced as if Optical Communications growth will remain at the same rate for the foreseeable future, and the Springboard benefits continue to flow with very limited friction. It's a reasonable base case, but there is very little room for disappointment. If the growth cools faster than expected or if the hyperscaler and enterprise customers pause their capex into 2026, then the earnings estimates will come under pressure quickly.

On the cost side, the execution risk hasn't disappeared. Springboard has delivered real progress in pricing discipline, mix, and operating leverage, but it only really delivers when the volumes are there. If costs remain high and the company can't hit its updated margin targets, that can hit sentiment harder than missing any revenue guidance.

In this case, what needs to happen for Springboard to remain as a key value driver is margins staying high even if one or two end markets soften. If Corning can show that it has lowered its break-even point on a structural basis, then I think the stock would have earned its premium.

Conversely, any evidence that Life Sciences and Specialty Materials can contribute any level of incremental growth will add some optional upside, in my view. This is because they smooth out any cyclicality from the other segments, and it means that Corning is less dependent on any single segment moving forward.

Q4 Preview: What I’m Watching When Results Hit In Two Weeks

Corning will report its Q4 2025 results on January 28, 2026, and since the company already guided Q4 in the Q3 release, the question for me is whether the story stays clean.

First, management guided to Q4 core sales of about $4.35B and core EPS of $0.68–$0.72, and it also said it expects to hit the 20% operating margin level in Q4. I want to see enterprise and GenAI-driven demand remain strong without the commentary shifting toward “timing” or “pull-forward.” Optical is the engine right now, so the tone that management uses here will matter as much as the number.

I don’t think that Display needs to grow sharply for this setup to work, but it needs to stay rational. I’m looking out for pricing and capacity discipline, so Display can keep doing its job as a stabilizer.

With Specialty Materials, I’ll be listening for how management frames timing and ramp expectations around that opportunity. Finally, the Hemlock and Emerging Growth segment has seen high demand lately, and given the narrative of strong demand for US-made solar products and solar wafer sales as Q4 drivers, I expect to see slightly more positive growth here, too.

Concluding Thoughts

Everything about the stock price indicates that the market has already factored in future results against today’s reality. I’m confident that Corning will hit Q4 guidance, given its track record over the past year, but that won’t be enough to make the stock look cheap. It needs more than that to justify the current price, and even more to open up any clear upside from here. That is why I’m initiating coverage with a Hold recommendation.

data.title: Gold Is No Longer An 'Alternative' - It's A Missing Strategic Allocation
data.description:
By Jeremy Schwartz, CFA

For most investors, zero gold exposure is not neutral—it's an active ~10% underweight relative to global market portfolios. And that gap is growing harder to justify as new portfolio tools become available that can enhance portfolio efficiency, diversification and optimization.

The Market Portfolio Tells an Uncomfortable Truth

A market-cap-weighted view of global investable assets implies roughly:

  • 52% equities
  • 32% fixed income
  • 16% alternatives, including:
    • ~12% gold
    • ~1.2% digital assets
    • 3% in real estate and other alternatives

The Liquid Market Portfolio

Source: Bloomberg, WisdomTree, 12/31/25. Market caps are shown in USD billion. You cannot invest directly in an index. Historical performance is not an indication of future performance and any investment may go down in value.

Passive investing has been gaining share within the asset classes, particularly equities. But the framework for looking at the investment opportunity set can be generalized to broader portfolio allocations. And the data is clear: a traditional 60/40 portfolio is structurally misaligned with the global opportunity set. It is overweight financial assets and underweight real assets, particularly gold.

Why this matters:

  • Most investors have 0–3% in gold and very little in diversified commodity allocations. I was recently looking at the total assets in diversified commodity strategies in the U.S. and there was a total of just $17 billion invested across the entire ETF industry that is over $13 trillion in size.
  • I sympathize with this under-allocation – for a decade following 2008, commodities went largely nowhere. But commodities showed some of their diversifying potential for hedging inflation and geopolitical risk following the Russian invasion of Ukraine.
  • Commodities are critical for powering much of the AI revolution and energy needs and rare earth metals are key focal points for the geopolitical developments around the globe. I think this low allocation to broad based commodity funds is a major mistake. Our pie chart above suggesting a neutral allocation at ½ of 1% is just based on the open interest of the futures market – and there is no easy way to suggest what should be neutral, but our historical work suggests as much as 10-15% allocations to commodities would better optimize portfolios and hedge key risks.
  • If investors hold 0-3% in gold, this is an active bet against inflation hedging, monetary debasement, and diversification.
  • International exposure is also typically too low in global allocations, again understandably because of the U.S. exceptionalism and tech dominance for the last 15 years. 2025 was the first breakout year for international in a long time, but gold did even better. Investors do not have to make bets on the euro, yen, or Chinese yuan in emerging markets to get protection from the falling purchasing power of the U.S. dollar. They can add gold in a capital-efficient way on top of U.S. stocks to accomplish similar outcomes.

The Real Constraint Isn't Conviction—It's Capital Efficiency

The common objection:

"I like gold, but I don't want to sell stocks or bonds that have more fundamental income streams to fund it."

That objection is rational. Selling productive assets to add a non-yielding one often feels like a step backward.

But WisdomTree pioneered an ETF product range that overcomes this challenge. You don't need to reallocate to gold from stocks or bonds if you can overlay it very capital efficiently.

That's the design philosophy behind our capital-efficient gold strategies:

  • Adds gold exposure via the futures market
  • Preserve core equity and fixed income
  • Improve portfolio resilience without forcing trade-offs

Gold's Long-Term Role: Keep up with Inflation, Not Growth Engine

Jeremy Siegel's Stocks for the Long Run provides critical historical context.

Since 1802: Gold Preserved Purchasing Power

  • Gold: ~0–1% real return
  • Stocks: ~7% real
  • Bonds: ~3% real

January 1802–December 2025

Source: Siegel, Jeremy, Stocks for the Long Run (2022) with updates to 2025. Past performance is not indicative of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Gold was never meant to beat equities over centuries. Its job was to keep up with inflation and protect purchasing power—which it did.

The purchasing power value of the U.S. dollar declined 93% over 200 years. Gold overcame that inflationary bias and returned positively after inflation.

But Siegel's 200-year study masks another interesting story over the last 50 years.

Since 1971: A Regime Shift Changed the Math

Once the gold standard ended, gold was liberated from artificially low pricing.

From 1971–2025 (real returns):

  • Stocks: 7.0%
  • Gold: 4.9%
  • Bonds: 2.8%
  • T-Bills: 0.5%
  • Dollar: -3.8

Gold didn't just hedge inflation—it outperformed bonds in real terms during the fiat-currency era.

January 1971–December 2025

Source: Siegel, Jeremy, Stocks for the Long Run (2022) with updates to 2025. Past performance is not indicative of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Forward Case for Gold: Why This Regime Persists

There is some natural fear today about adding to gold after chasing an enormous performance run. But there are some structural tailwinds for continued gold allocations.

1. Global Debt Dynamics

  • Sovereign debt-to-GDP ratios remain historically high
  • Financial repression (inflation) while keeping rates low is the path of least resistance. The way countries have managed debt levels across time is to allow inflation.
  • Gold benefits when real rates are capped

2. Dollar Debasement Risk

  • Persistent fiscal deficits provide some concern for the bond market and gold overlays can provide diversification from pure bond allocations.
  • Long-term erosion of fiat purchasing power even without crisis

3. Central Banks Are Voting with Their Balance Sheets

  • Emerging market central banks continue to accumulate gold. There are many reasons, but the geopolitical stress in which Russia was caught off from its U.S. dollar reserves provides motivation for holding less U.S. Treasuries and more real assets.
  • This is price-insensitive, structural demand and we see central banks continuing to accumulate, particularly China

Gold doesn't require catastrophe; it simply requires policy constraints and geopolitical uncertainties.

How We Think About Implementation: Two use cases. Two tools. One philosophy.

GDE — Strategic Gold, Capital-Efficient

  • Designed as a long-term core holding to replace other equity funds
  • Adds gold futures exposure on top of U.S. large-cap equities
  • Seeks diversification and inflation resilience without selling core stock positions.

Use case: Investors who believe gold belongs strategically in portfolios, but don't want to give up equity exposure to get it.

GDMN — Tactical Conviction in Gold & Miners

  • Higher-octane exposure. This was among WisdomTree and the industry's best-performing Funds in 2025 as miners rebounded.
  • Leans into gold miners, which historically delivered subpar returns. Miners have a reputation for being poorly managed capital allocators. This run in gold prices came while energy prices remained low. Some of the current geopolitics looks to provide a ceiling for oil prices (particularly around Venezuelan oil that we expect to become better globally supplied in coming years). This gives a positive backdrop for gold miner margins.
  • This Fund is more tactical and best suited for investors with a strong near-term bullish view and want more torque to the trade.
  • Gold miner flows were very modest to non-existent in 2025, despite strong performance.

Use case: When fundamentals, flows, or macro regime shifts favor gold leadership.

Bottom Line

  • Zero gold allocation in a portfolio is not neutral, it's an under-weight bet against gold.
  • The global market portfolio argues for meaningfully higher real-asset exposure.
  • History shows gold's value is regime-dependent, and there are some positive forces supporting gold over the coming years.
  • Capital efficiency matters. You shouldn't have to dismantle your portfolio to fix its biggest blind spot.

Adding gold doesn't have to be about replacing stocks or bonds. It's about making portfolios more complete efficiently.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile. The Funds’ investment strategy will also require it to redeem shares for cash or to otherwise include cash as part of its redemption proceeds, which may cause the Fund to recognize capital gains.

The Fund is actively managed and invests in U.S listed gold futures and U.S. equity securities. The Fund’s use of U.S. listed gold futures contracts will give rise to leverage, magnifying gains and losses and causing the Fund to be more volatile than if it had not been leveraged. Moreover, the price movements in gold and gold futures contracts may fluctuate quickly and dramatically, and have a historically low correlation with the returns of the stock and bond markets. U.S. equity securities, such as common stocks, are subject to market, economic and business risks that may cause their prices to fluctuate.

The Fund is actively managed and invests in U.S.-listed gold futures and global equity securities issued by companies that derive at least 50% of their revenue from the gold mining business (“Gold Miners”). The Fund’s use of U.S.-listed gold futures contracts will give rise to leverage, magnifying gains and losses and causing the Fund to be more volatile than if it had not been leveraged. Moreover, the price movements in gold and gold futures contracts may fluctuate quickly and dramatically, and have a historically low correlation with the returns of the stock and bond markets. By investing in the equity securities of Gold Miners, the Fund may be susceptible to financial, economic, political, or market events that impact the gold mining sub-industry, including commodity prices and the success of exploration projects. The Fund may invest a significant portion of its assets in the securities of companies of a single country or region, including emerging markets, and thus, the Fund is more likely to be impacted by events and political, economic, or regulatory conditions affecting that country or region, or emerging markets generally.

Jeremy Schwartz, CFA, Global Chief Investment Officer

Jeremy Schwartz has served as our Global Chief Investment Officer since November 2021 and leads WisdomTree’s investment strategy team in the construction of WisdomTree’s equity Indexes, quantitative active strategies and multi-asset Model Portfolios. Jeremy joined WisdomTree in May 2005 as a Senior Analyst, adding Deputy Director of Research to his responsibilities in February 2007. He served as Director of Research from October 2008 to October 2018 and as Global Head of Research from November 2018 to November 2021. Before joining WisdomTree, he was a head research assistant for Professor Jeremy Siegel and, in 2022, became his co-author on the sixth edition of the book Stocks for the Long Run. Jeremy is also co-author of the Financial Analysts Journal paper “What Happened to the Original Stocks in the S&P 500?” He received his B.S. in economics from The Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania and hosts the Wharton Business Radio program Behind the Markets on SiriusXM 132. Jeremy is a member of the CFA Society of Philadelphia.

Original Post

data.title: BXMX: Efficient Income Fund To Hedge An S&P 500 Decline
data.description:
Overview

Despite market indices continuing to rise to new all-time highs, Nuveen S&P 500 Buy-Write Income Fund (BXMX) continues to trade at a discounted valuation. When I previously covered BXMX, I issued a buy rating due to the potential to collect tax-efficient income for long-term income investors. Since my last coverage, BXMX's total return has slightly outpaced the S&P 500 (SPX). Now that we've crossed into the new year, I wanted to revisit BXMX to assess its valuation and dig deeper into its performance insights for investors that want to simply buy-and-hold this fund.

Since BXMX is uniquely structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the underlying value of its assets. As a result, BXMX continues to trade at one of the deepest discounts to NAV valuations over the last ten-year period. BXMX currently trades at a discount to NAV of 9.25%, which is more attractive than its 5-year historical average discount to NAV of 5.51%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, this helps visualize just how attractively valued the fund is at this time.

BXMX has the potential to be a great long-term holding since it directly provides exposure to the constituents of the S&P 500. BXMX offers a high starting dividend yield of about 7.4% at this time, and the fund has a strong history of consistent payouts. Despite the fund specifically aiming to replicate the S&P 500 Index, the emphasis on income generation means that there are some tradeoffs that investors must consider. For instance, BXMX is very likely to underperform traditional index-tracking ETFs over a longer holding period. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that BXMX implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund overview, BXMX has total managed assets of $1.69B that are spread across a diverse pool of equities. The primary goal of the fund is to provide attractive total returns while simultaneously demonstrating less volatility than the S&P 500. In order to achieve this goal, the fund sells index call options covering ~100% of the fund's equity portfolio value. So BXMX is essentially writing options and using the option premiums collected to fund the distributions being paid.

Digging deeper into the option strategy, BXMX utilizes ATM (at-the-money) call options, which means that it selects a strike price that is at or very near the current market price of the index. The benefit of this strategy is that an ATM approach can generate a higher level of premiums than an OTM (out-of-the-money) approach. The downside is that an ATM approach severely caps the upside growth compared to an OTM approach, which selects strike prices above the current market price of the index and allows for greater upside movement.

So if the S&P 500 Index is currently trading around ~$6,980, BXMX may write an option and select a strike price of $6,990. In exchange, the fund receives a premium, but this means that any growth above the strike price of $6,990 will be sacrificed. This can be an issue during strong market rallies and lead to underperformance.

Since the fund is essentially made up of constituents of the index, the sector allocation looks very similar. As we can see below, BXMX is most heavily allocated to Information Technology companies at 33.4% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Financials and Communication Services, which accounts for 14% and 10.4% of assets, respectively.

When looking at the top holdings, we can see that BXMX provides direct equity exposure to some of the highest-quality growth companies in the world. For instance, the largest position is currently Nvidia (NVDA) at 7.4% of assets. This is followed by many other notable names, such as Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Broadcom (AVGO), just to name a few. With BXMX, investors are able to get exposure to the Mag 7 companies while collecting an income level that isn't typically possible when investing in these companies directly.

Performance Insights & Risks

Despite the fund providing direct exposure to these growth companies, investors need to limit their expectations around the fund's ability to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation. Since an option writing strategy is part of the process, the fund will cap upside growth. Additionally, BXMX also needs to sell off portions of the upside growth of its own holdings in order to support distributions. So unlike traditional ETFs, BXMX isn't structured to simply let its winners run uninterrupted over time.

To demonstrate this vulnerability, we can refer to the fund's performance over the last decade. As we can see below, BXMX's share price has only increased by about 19.7% over the last ten years. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 144.6% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that roughly 90% of their total return will come from the fund's ability to maintain a consistent rate of high payouts, while the remaining 10% will come from actual price growth over time.

However, the inclusion of an option writing strategy has its benefits. As stated in the fund's objective, BXMX aims to provide less volatility than the index, and it usually achieves this. A good demonstration of this took place throughout 2022's market decline. Market indices declined in response to record-breaking inflationary data, uncertainty around the pandemic, and shifts in the labor market. The SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) saw a total loss of 18.17% over the course of 2022. Although BXMX also saw losses during the year, the high distributions received were able to soften the blow and resulted in a total loss of only 7.15% over the same time. Even from a price perspective, BXMX has proven its ability to be an efficient defensive position during unfavorable market conditions.

Although I am bullish on the outlook of the market for 2026, BXMX can be a great hedge against a shift in sentiment. Market momentum continues to be carried by the expansion of margins and earnings within the technology sector, mostly driven by optimism around AI. However, optimism in the technology sector can fade rapidly due to concerns around high capex spending, competition from global competitors stealing market share, and the potential for earnings growth to slow. So if you don't share the same optimism for 2026, BXMX is a great way to position yourself to benefit from the uncertainty.

However, BXMX is likely to underperform if the positive market momentum does continue. By focusing on income generation, BXMX has severely underperformed SPY over the last decade. This can be attributed to the combination of the option writing strategy and large distributions that are paid out. Therefore, I want to warn young dividend investors that focusing on income can lead to underperformance over the long term.

Dividend Utility

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.2725 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.4%. Looking back over the fund's dividend history, we can see that BXMX has a great track record of keeping payouts within a consistent range. However, investors looking for a traditional sense of dividend growth over time may be disappointed with the fund's lack of raises. I believe this is acceptable since investors are already being paid such a high dividend yield upfront.

Additionally, I believe that the fund's history of consistent payouts is still to be admired. The consistency essentially enables long-term investors to create their own dividend growth by reinvesting the income received or by adding new capital over time. This can be ideal for an investor who is roughly five years away from retirement age and would like to start building a supplemental income stream to help support lifestyle expenses.

In order to demonstrate how this could benefit retired investors, I ran a back test of an original $100,000 investment at the start of 2016. This assumes that no additional capital was ever invested after the initial $100K. Additionally, the back test also assumes that all dividends received were reinvested back into the accumulation of more BXMX shares. As we can see by the results below, investors that simply collected and reinvested would have been able to more than double their dividend income effortlessly:

2016 Dividend Income: $7,142

2025 Dividend Income: $15,844

Lastly, an added bonus to BXMX's distribution is that they have the potential to remain tax-efficient. Based on the latest available Section 19(a) notice, 77.5% of the distributions paid YTD were classified as return of capital. Return of capital distributions are considered to be tax-efficient because the distributions aren't directly taxed as income. Instead, return of capital distributions reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. So if you never plan to sell a position like this, you can continue to defer those taxes.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that BXMX is still a buy today. The fund trades at one of the deepest discounts to NAV levels over the last decade and has the ability to provide direct exposure to constituents of the S&P 500 Index. However, the inclusion of an option writing strategy means that BXMX is positioned to underperform if the market's positive momentum continues over the course of 2026. Conversely, BXMX can be an efficient hedge against a sentiment shift in the market since the fund has proven to remain more resilient than traditional ETFs during market declines. Lastly, BXMX has an excellent history of consistent dividends, and the limited tax burden increases the appeal for long-term investors.

data.title: Pour Decisions And Puff Choices: 2026 Is Dishing Out Health Conscious Choices
data.description:
The new year, new me ethos is not just a personal mantra – it’s a business imperative. As consumer preferences shift toward healthier and more sustainable choices around beverages and cigarettes, supply chains adapt.

Wine: Non-alcoholic trends gain momentum

The wine industry is bracing for another year of change, with non-alcoholic wines poised to become a mainstream category. This follows a pattern seen previously in the beer sector, where non-alcoholic brews surged in popularity since 2024. In the EU, non-alcoholic wine exports (including intra-EU trade) reached 2.35 billion liters in the 12 months to September 2025, representing 34.9% of alcoholic wine volumes. However, the sector remains largely inward-focused, with only 27.1% of non-alcoholic wine exports heading beyond EU borders, compared to 41.9% for alcoholic wines.

Growth in non-alcoholic wine exports was a modest 5.1% year over year in Q3 2025. The US market, while showing a 37.8% rebound in Q3, remains well below 2023 levels, highlighting the complexities of inventory and shipping strategies for European producers. As technology for dealcoholization improves and more vintners enter the space, we expect further shifts in global wine supply chains.

Spirits: Whiskey faces headwinds amid changing tastes

The spirits sector is not immune to disruption. Suntory Holdings Ltd.’s decision to pause production at a Kentucky distillery underscores the impact of declining US whiskey exports, which fell 23.8% year over year in Q3 2025. Shipments to the EU were particularly hard-hit, plunging 43.3% despite the suspension of proposed EU tariffs on US whiskey.

UK whisky exporters also faced challenges, with global sales down 2.5% in the first half of 2025 and shipments to the US falling 11.4% in Q3 YOY. Meanwhile, US imports of whiskies from Canada and Ireland grew by 11.1% and 16.2%, respectively, even as these products attracted higher tariffs. These trends underscore the importance of agile supply chain planning in the face of shifting consumer tastes and trade policies.

Nicotine products: Alternatives drive growth

Health-conscious consumers, particularly younger generations, are reshaping the nicotine market. The UK, for example, has seen a rise in nicotine pouch usage – especially among men aged 16–24 – following a ban on electronic vapes. Globally, exports of nicotine products for oral intake grew 17.1% in the year to October 2025, with Sweden, Hungary, and Denmark leading production.

The EU remains the largest market for these alternatives, accounting for 47.3% of global imports, followed by the US (13.8%) and Canada (10.4%). While cigarette imports into the EU reached US$19.7 billion (up 5.2% year over year), nicotine pouch imports surged 43.0% to US$1.9 billion, and e-cigarette imports declined 11.8% to US$2.9 billion.

Electronic cigarette trade has been volatile, with a 6.6% year-over-year increase in global exports in the 12 months to October 2025, following a contraction in 2024. The EU and Japan remain key markets, but regulatory scrutiny is likely to persist as new alternatives emerge.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: New Gold-ETF Data: Record Inflows Fuel Metal's Best Year Since 1979
data.description:
Gold closed out 2025 in runaway mode, notching fresh all-time highs on 53 trading days and sprinting to an intraday peak of $4,514 per ounce on the year’s final Friday. The blistering rally ignited a historic rush into physically backed exchange-traded funds (ETFs), which absorbed a record $89 billion and more than doubled global assets under management to an unprecedented $559 billion. Seven straight months of inflows capped the metal’s strongest annual performance since 1979, underscoring how nervous capital is seeking shelter from policy missteps, geopolitical flare-ups, and an increasingly frothy equity market.

North American investors led the charge, accounting for $51 billion—about 57 percent—of total ETF inflows. Assets and tonnage for the region’s funds finished the year at all-time highs, with flagship vehicles such as SPDR Gold Shares raking in $3.6 billion in December alone. Asian demand was nearly as eye-catching: regional products gathered $25 billion, a larger haul than all net buying from 2007 through 2024 combined, while Indian funds strung together eight consecutive months of additions. Even Europe, after two years of redemptions, reversed course with a $12 billion splash led by the United Kingdom and Switzerland.

The Federal Reserve’s December “hawkish” quarter-point rate cut—paired with a quiet restart of Treasury bill purchases—only hardened bullion’s allure. While officials framed the move as prudent fine-tuning, markets saw stealth easing and a precursor to deeper liquidity injections once a new Fed Chair takes the helm in early 2026. Such policy zigzags have long been kryptonite for savers, inflating asset bubbles and eroding purchasing power. It is little wonder that many portfolio managers are reaching for the one asset Washington cannot print.

Trading activity told the same story. Average daily gold turnover leapt 56 percent year-on-year to $361 billion, split almost evenly between over-the-counter trades and exchange contracts, with ETF dealing contributing another $7 billion. COMEX net-long positioning ended December at 683 tonnes—up 15 percent on the month—yet still sat 30 percent below end-2024 levels, suggesting room for more speculative fuel if policy or politics delivers another spark.

And political sparks were plentiful. President Trump’s December 26th airstrikes against Islamic State targets in Nigeria, plus simmering tensions with Venezuela, magnified safe-haven demand. Meanwhile, whispers of an AI-driven stock bubble, reminiscent of 1999’s dot-com delusion, kept volatility hedgers busy. In December alone, global ETFs soaked up $10 billion, with North America contributing $6.2 billion, Asia $2.5 billion, and Europe $1 billion.

2025’s message was clear: investors trust ounces over a degrading dollar. Unless policymakers rediscover fiscal restraint and sound-money principles, the yellow metal’s magnetism will only increase in 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Markets Brush Off U.S. Geopolitical, Policy Actions As Rude Awakening Looms
data.description:
Just over two weeks into 2026, President Donald Trump has approved the capture of Venezuela's president, made fresh threats against Iran, again pushed to take over Greenland, called for lower credit card and mortgage rates, and continued to threaten the Federal Reserve's independence.

Financial markets shrugged. Even with the torrent of risks out of the White House to upend long-standing geopolitics and the economy, US stocks soared to all-time highs, government bond yields barely budged, and the dollar moved little within the narrow range it has been in for months.

"There are interesting things going on around the world, of course, but these tend to be short-lived for markets," said JP Coviello, Citi Wealth's head of portfolio strategy, in an interview. "We fall back on fundamentals, which is where is earnings growth coming from and where is it likely to continue to come from."

As global conflicts have escalated, few have had much market impact, said Tyler Richey, a co-editor with Sevens Report Research. "It's as if markets have grown numb to geopolitical influence," Richey said.

Investors began 2026 with an overall complacent mindset, pursuing their instincts in equities to continue to buy on any dips in the market. Investor enthusiasm has been fueled by expectations for significant earnings growth, a soft landing again avoiding a recession and a third year of strong market gains, said Richey.

Richey described the market as being in the midst of a "Rip Van Winkle" period. "It's accustomed to easy money, low-to-negative real rates, and a soft Fed afraid of breaking anything loose in the financial system," Richey said. The market could be in for a "rude awakening" this year as it continues to underestimate risks, Richey said.

Much of the lack of recent movement in markets could be due to the "Trump Put," a belief that if the market stumbles too significantly, the president will reverse course on any action that could be negatively impacting stocks, said Michael O'Rourke, chief market strategist with JonesTrading. But the efficacy of the Trump Put could be weakening, O'Rourke said.

"The barrage of policy moves might finally begin to take a toll here," O'Rourke said. "We are likely to hit a point where investors will start mild profit taking in order to allow the numerous initiatives to process. The president's populist attacks on corporate America are beginning to garner negative reactions."

Much of the news out of the White House is unprecedented and poses significant potential consequences for the US and the world, but market strategists said much of it has no immediate, clear impact on companies' cash flows, returns on assets, or profitability.

"These issues have not really impacted the fundamentals," said Michael Crook, chief investment officer at Mill Creek Capital. "The fundamentals are still good."

While efforts to undermine the Fed's independence could upend bond yields if ultimately successful or further military actions could hinder economic growth, the outcome and impacts remain unclear, said Torsten Slok, chief economist at Apollo Global Management.

For now, there has been no clear impact on corporate earnings, which has left financial markets mostly unaffected. "We just don't know where this is going," Slok said. "A lot of these things are still so uncertain."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dana Incorporated: Bullish About Business Divestment And Margin Expansion
data.description:
I'm still a bull on Dana Incorporated (DAN).

This considers its recent business portfolio restructuring and profitability enhancement moves. The stock is also trading at an appealing valuation compared with its peers.

Its encouraging FY24 guide was the subject of my prior February 23, 2024, update.

Off-Highway Division Exit Is Value-Enhancing

At the beginning of this year, DAN revealed that the "sale of the Off-Highway/OH business to Allison Transmission Holdings, Inc. (ALSN) for $2.7 billion" was concluded.

I have a positive opinion of the above-mentioned deal for a number of reasons.

I see a narrowing of the holding company discount assigned to DAN's shares.

The group took part in "Barclays (BCS) 16th Annual Global Automotive and Mobility Tech Conference" on Nov. 19 last year. During the BCS forum, it was emphasized that the divestiture will "radically simplify the business with 2 end markets."

Investors should judge the firm to be an automotive pure-play, justifying richer valuations post-transaction, in my view.

OH's operations involve "a wide range of applications in construction, forestry, agriculture, specialty, aftermarket, industrial, and mining segments," according to ALSN's announcement. Ex-OH, DAN is solely focused on "Light Vehicles/LVs" and "Commercial Vehicles/CVs."

Debt pay-down and increased shareholder-friendliness are also likely to trigger a re-rating of DAN's stock. These will be funded by OH's divestment proceeds.

It's planning for "$2 billion of deleveraging" as per UBS Group's (UBS) "Global Industrials and Transportation Conference" commentary on December 3 last year. That's aligned with the goal of cutting the net "Debt-to-EBITDA/Leverage" metric from 4.3x (source: S&P Capital IQ) as of the end-3Q25 to 1.0x or lower for the future.

Separately, DAN has initiated a fresh $1,000 million cash distribution program with its balance sheet getting stronger.

This led to its FY25 cash distributions rising by +8% from the initial target of $600 million to the revised $0.65 billion.

The remaining $350 million will be paid out in '26/27. That translates into a forward annualized stockholder yield of 5.4%.

Expecting Better Operating Profitability

DAN's 3Q2025 EBITDA-to-sales was 8.5%. The mid-point of its full-year FY25 guidance is 7.95%.

Looking ahead, the company is anticipating a "10% to 10.5% EBITDA margin" for FY26 based on the UBS event disclosures.

My take is that a 2.0-2.5 ppts (percentage points) EBITDA-to-revenue improvement for the current year is doable.

I'm impressed with the progress of DAN's ongoing $0.31 billion expense optimization exercise.

At the 3Q25 briefing in late-Oct of the previous year, it shared that "$245 million" of "run rate cost savings" have already been realized. That represents almost four-fifths (245/310) of the target.

Also, parting ways with OH helps to boost group-level margins.

DAN acknowledged at UBS's conference that "Off-Highway" was "our highest working capital-intensive business."

Moving forward, it can redirect cash flow previously utilized from OH's inventories and receivables elsewhere. The additional capital available for deployment in higher-margin areas will impact the firm's earnings in a good way.

There's around $0.04 billion of yearly fixed overhead, which was used to support the "Off-Highway" unit in the past. DAN aims to get rid of these outlays by the end of FY26.

The spotlight is on the group's changed financial priorities as well.

During the mid-Nov '25 BCS event, DAN's CFO stressed that "I'm happy to have a slightly smaller top line and a much more robust cash flow and margin profile going forward."

It's clear that the enterprise is willing to trade off a slower pace of growth for stronger operational profits. It is thinking of taking actions such as terminating unprofitable contracts and removing low-return "Stock Keeping Units/SKUs."

DAN is also a beneficiary of favorable industry trends.

BCS's analyst highlighted at the Nov. 19, 2025, forum "automakers are talking about improved mix" with a shift towards "larger vehicles or different engine variants."

In response, DAN said that its clients "obviously charge a higher price" for these superior offerings cited above. It noted, "we look to do the same thing with our product (supplied) to them." This suggests that there are price-hiking opportunities for the firm.

You Should Consider These Risks

It's possible that DAN takes a longer-than-expected period of time to repay borrowings. It may also return a lower-than-anticipated amount of capital to shareholders.

A failure to deliver a double-digit EBITDA-to-turnover will leave investors disappointed. This can happen due to surges in expenses or cost-down pressures exerted by its customers.

My Concluding Remarks

DAN stays a "Buy"-rated name.

The market values this name at an FY2026 EV/EBITDA of 4.8 times as per S&P Capital IQ.

The mean forward EBITDA multiple for automotive suppliers, BorgWarner (BWA), Magna (MGA), Aptiv (APTV), Lear (LEA), and Autoliv (ALV) is 6.2x.

DAN's EBITDA expansion potential is +29% (6.2/4.8) benchmarked with its listed comparables. A more demanding valuation is warranted with the positives pertaining to OH's sale and its margin outlook.

data.title: A Quiet Start To 2026 Macro - If You Ignore Everything That's Happening
data.description:
It’s been a slow start to the year. Not much macro news to capture eyeballs or Wall Street’s attention. Oh, just a regime overthrow in Venezuela, capital controls on defense-industry companies, possible QE-like activity in the mortgage-backed securities market, proposed caps on credit card interest rates, and a criminal-indictment threat forced upon Fed Chair Jerome Powell. Not much to write home about, yeah?

Indeed, it has been a hectic beginning to 2026. Amid so many macro curveballs and noise stemming from the White House, it has also been busy at the corporate level.

Conference Season Roundup

CEOs are not only tiptoeing around presidential comments and social media posts, but they are also presenting strategy updates and industry outlooks at major corporate conferences this week and throughout the month.

CES was reinvigorating for consumer tech last week, and right now we have data out from the National Retail Federation’s Retail’s Big Show happening in New York City, the ICR 29th Annual Conference in Orlando, and perhaps the most widely followed gathering of all - the JP Morgan 44th Annual Healthcare Conference in San Francisco.

Earnings Season Enters the Chat

Amid the slide decks and fireside chats, Q4 earnings reports are hitting left and right. JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and The Bank of New York Mellon (BK) posted results this past Tuesday, followed by Bank of America (BAC), Wells Fargo (WFC), and Citigroup (C) earlier on Wednesday.

Goldman Sachs (GS) and Morgan Stanley (MS) serve up revenue and profit figures on Thursday morning. The season broadens out to other cyclical sectors next week before big tech and AI snag the spotlight later in January.

Hello Data, My Old Friend

Rounding out the triad of volatility catalysts this week? Economic data.

Traders are back in the swing of things following the longest U.S. government shutdown in history - the December jobs report printed last Friday, CPI hit this past Tuesday, PPI and retail sales were released this morning, while manufacturing and housing data arrive Friday. With a chunk of domestic macro data in the bag, investors have a clearer view of the backdrop as we head into the heart of the reporting season.

According to our Economic Calendar, the New York Empire Manufacturing Index will be published on Thursday morning. This one often flies under the radar, but it may be worth putting under the microscope.

It’s a January survey data point, providing a timely vibes check on the real economy in the Northeast region. Paired with the Philly Fed Business Outlook, released at the same time, market participants will get a pulse on optimism (or lack thereof) amid a soft labor environment and ongoing inflation concerns.

Going forward, business tax breaks could buoy corner-office sentiment, while record-high tax refund checks hitting consumers’ accounts starting next month just might improve households’ feelings on the economy... so long as jobs don’t fall off a cliff.

The Labor Market Stalemate Continues

Speaking of the employment situation, initial jobless claims come at the usual time on Thursday, also before the bell. The “no-hire, no-fire” labor market presses on, so we expect the number of first-time applications for unemployment benefits to remain low. Continuing claims, always published an extra week in arrears, could still be noisy from the holidays.

As it stands, the December nonfarm payrolls report was sort of a choose-your-own-adventure. The headline 50,000 jobs gain was below Street estimates, but the unemployment rate dropped 16 basis points to 4.375% from November.

The bears can point to steep downward revisions to October–November payrolls, while the bulls hang their hats on broader labor data (including private-sector reports), possibly suggesting the softening employment trend is at or near its trough.

The Fed: Wait and See

What does it all mean for investors? Combine the December job numbers, inflation updates, and retail sales gain, and we get a recipe for the Fed to possibly stay on hold through Powell’s term as Fed chief (May 15).

The Treasury market sees about two quarter-point rate cuts from now to year-end, and there will undoubtedly be added pressure on whoever the new chair is to curry enough favor among the 11 other FOMC voting members for a June cut.

The January 28 Fed interest rate decision will presumably come with extra fireworks, too. After Powell’s unprecedented direct video response to the federal subpoena last Sunday night, he will no doubt be probed about his thoughts and plans in light of renewed attacks from the White House. To be clear, President Trump denied knowledge of the Department of Justice’s inquiry. Through it all, both stock and bond volatility gauges are subdued.

Markets Shaking It All Off

In fact, markets have taken earnings, data, and policy intrigue in stride since the summer. The Dow Jones Industrial Average is now near 50,000, the S&P 500 is a stone’s throw from 7,000, and international indices press to record highs. The bond market seems to buy into the “TACO Trade” for the moment, but precious metals’ torrid rally underscores that all may not be well regarding fiscal and monetary matters.

For now, focusing on actual data and CEO commentary might be the best approach.

The Bottom Line

The macro setting is being set for 2026. Official economic reports have rolled in as scheduled after a Q4 data vacuum. At the same time, a decent start to the earnings season reminds investors that companies can still deliver strong profit growth despite theatrics playing out in Washington, DC. Keep both the data deck and earnings calendar on your radar in the weeks ahead.

Original Post
